content.text: Least-Privilege Identities

The principle of least privilege states that an identity should have access to only the resources, actions, and scopes it genuinely needs — no more. In Azure, this translates to three concrete decisions: which role to assign, at which scope to assign it, and whether ABAC conditions can restrict it further. Getting these right limits the blast radius when a credential is compromised or a workload is misconfigured.

attrs.alt: Least privilege scope funnel: over-privileged vs just-enough role assignments

content.text: The Three Axes of Least Privilege in Azure RBAC

Every role assignment has three components: a principal (the identity), a role definition (the set of allowed actions), and a scope (the resource tree it applies to). Least privilege means choosing the narrowest role and the narrowest scope that satisfies the workload's actual needs.
Axis
Broad (avoid)
Narrow (target)
Role
Contributor or Owner
Purpose-built data plane role (e.g. Storage Blob Data Reader)
Scope
Subscription or Management Group
Resource Group, individual resource, or container/queue
ABAC condition
None
Tag filter, blob path prefix, or queue name match

Owner and Contributor are management-plane roles that can create new role assignments or modify infrastructure. A workload that only needs to read blobs should never hold either of these. Data-plane roles (Storage Blob Data Reader, Key Vault Secrets User) restrict access to data operations only.
Choosing the Right Built-In Role

Azure provides hundreds of built-in roles. For managed identities and service principals, the data plane roles are almost always the right choice. Management plane roles (Contributor, Owner) grant the ability to change resource configuration and create new role assignments — privileges that application workloads almost never need.
Common data-plane roles for workloads
Resource
Action needed
Role to assign
Azure Blob Storage
Read objects
Storage Blob Data Reader
Azure Blob Storage
Read + write objects
Storage Blob Data Contributor
Azure Queue Storage
Send + receive messages
Storage Queue Data Contributor
Azure Key Vault (RBAC mode)
Read specific secrets
Key Vault Secrets User
Azure Key Vault (RBAC mode)
Read + write secrets
Key Vault Secrets Officer
Azure Service Bus
Send messages
Azure Service Bus Data Sender
Azure Service Bus
Receive messages
Azure Service Bus Data Receiver
Azure Event Hubs
Produce events
Azure Event Hubs Data Sender
Azure Event Hubs
Consume events
Azure Event Hubs Data Receiver
Azure Cosmos DB
Read data
Cosmos DB Built-in Data Reader
Scoping Role Assignments Narrowly

A role assignment at the subscription scope grants access to every resource in the subscription, including resources created in the future. Always assign at the narrowest scope that satisfies the requirement.
Scope hierarchy (broadest to narrowest)
Management Group — applies to multiple subscriptions; avoid for workload identities
Subscription — applies to all resource groups and resources in the subscription
Resource Group — applies to all resources in the group; acceptable for workloads that genuinely need access to an entire group
Resource — applies to a single storage account, Key Vault, Service Bus namespace, etc.
Sub-resource — applies to a single blob container, queue, or topic (supported for Storage and Service Bus)

# Bad: assign at subscription scope
az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id $PRINCIPAL_ID \
  --role 'Storage Blob Data Reader' \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB

# Better: assign at resource group scope
az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id $PRINCIPAL_ID \
  --role 'Storage Blob Data Reader' \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups/myRG

# Best: assign at container scope
az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id $PRINCIPAL_ID \
  --role 'Storage Blob Data Reader' \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/mysa/blobServices/default/containers/app-data
ABAC Conditions: Restricting Within a Role

Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) conditions are expressions attached to a role assignment that further restrict which objects the principal can access, even within a container. ABAC conditions are evaluated at authorization time and do not require a custom role.
Restrict to blobs with a specific tag

# Grant read access only to blobs tagged environment=production
az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id $PRINCIPAL_ID \
  --role 'Storage Blob Data Reader' \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/mysa/blobServices/default/containers/shared-data \
  --condition '(
    (
      !(ActionMatches{"Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/blobServices/containers/blobs/read"})
    )
    OR
    (
      @Resource[Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/blobServices/containers/blobs/tags:environment<\$key_case_insensitive\$>] StringEqualsIgnoreCase "production"
    )
  )' \
  --condition-version '2.0'
Restrict Key Vault secret access to a specific secret name

# Grant access to exactly one secret by name
az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id $PRINCIPAL_ID \
  --role 'Key Vault Secrets User' \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/myKV \
  --condition '(
    (
      !(ActionMatches{"Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/secrets/getSecret/action"})
      AND
      !(ActionMatches{"Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/secrets/readMetadata/action"})
    )
    OR
    (
      @Resource[Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/secrets:name] StringEquals "db-password"
    )
  )' \
  --condition-version '2.0'
Custom Roles for Precise Action Sets

When no built-in role matches the exact set of actions a workload needs, create a custom role. Custom roles define an explicit allowlist of actions and can be scoped to a subscription or management group. They are particularly useful when a workload needs a mix of actions that spans multiple built-in roles but should not have all the actions in either.

{
  "Name": "Queue Message Processor",
  "Description": "Read and complete messages from a specific Service Bus queue",
  "Actions": [],
  "NotActions": [],
  "DataActions": [
    "Microsoft.ServiceBus/namespaces/messages/receive/action",
    "Microsoft.ServiceBus/namespaces/messages/complete/action"
  ],
  "NotDataActions": [],
  "AssignableScopes": [
    "/subscriptions/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000"
  ]
}

# Create the custom role from the JSON definition
az role definition create --role-definition @custom-queue-role.json

# Assign it at the queue scope (not namespace scope)
az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id $PRINCIPAL_ID \
  --role 'Queue Message Processor' \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.ServiceBus/namespaces/myNS/queues/orders
Auditing Effective Permissions

Before assigning a role, verify what permissions the identity already has — inheritance from resource groups and subscriptions can result in more access than intended. After assigning, audit the effective role assignments regularly to remove stale or over-broad assignments.
List all role assignments for an identity

# Show all role assignments for a specific principal
az role assignment list \
  --assignee $PRINCIPAL_ID \
  --all \
  --query '[].{Role:roleDefinitionName, Scope:scope}' \
  -o table

# Show effective permissions at a specific scope (includes inherited)
az role assignment list \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups/myRG \
  --query '[?principalId==`'"$PRINCIPAL_ID"'`].{Role:roleDefinitionName, Scope:scope}' \
  -o table
KQL: find identities with Owner or Contributor at subscription scope

// Detect broad management-plane role assignments
AzureActivity
| where OperationNameValue == 'MICROSOFT.AUTHORIZATION/ROLEASSIGNMENTS/WRITE'
| where ActivityStatusValue == 'Success'
| extend Props = todynamic(Properties)
| extend RoleDefId = tostring(Props.requestbody)
| where RoleDefId contains '8e3af657-a8ff-443c-a75c-2fe8c4bcb635'  // Owner
    or RoleDefId contains 'b24988ac-6180-42a0-ab88-20f7382dd24c'  // Contributor
| where ResourceGroup == ''  // subscription-level (no RG in scope)
| project TimeGenerated, Caller, ResourceId, RoleDefId
| order by TimeGenerated desc
Entra ID Application Permissions: Least Privilege for Microsoft Graph

Service principals that call Microsoft Graph need application permissions (not delegated). The default tendency is to request broad permissions (User.Read.All, Directory.Read.All) because they cover every use case. Instead, request only the specific permissions the workload needs and prefer the least-permissive variant.
Over-broad permission
Narrower alternative
Use when
User.Read.All
User.ReadBasic.All
Only need name, email, and photo
Directory.Read.All
GroupMember.Read.All
Only need to check group membership
Mail.ReadWrite
Mail.Send
Workload only sends email, never reads inbox
Files.ReadWrite.All
Files.ReadWrite (delegated only)
Service-to-service file ops unavoidable — scope to specific site

# Grant only Mail.Send instead of Mail.ReadWrite
APP_ID='00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'
GRAPH_APP_ID='00000003-0000-0000-c000-000000000000'

# Find the permission ID for Mail.Send
MAIL_SEND_ID=$(az ad sp show --id $GRAPH_APP_ID \
  --query "appRoles[?value=='Mail.Send'].id" -o tsv)

# Add the application permission
az ad app permission add \
  --id $APP_ID \
  --api $GRAPH_APP_ID \
  --api-permissions $MAIL_SEND_ID=Role

# Admin consent required for application permissions
az ad app permission admin-consent --id $APP_ID
Least-Privilege Checklist
Use data-plane roles (Reader, User, Sender) instead of management-plane roles (Contributor, Owner) for all workloads.
Assign at the narrowest possible scope: container or queue before resource, resource before resource group, resource group before subscription.
Apply ABAC conditions when multiple workloads share a container or vault but should not see each other's objects.
Create custom roles when no built-in role exactly matches the required action set.
Audit effective permissions with az role assignment list --all before and after any deployment.
Review and remove stale role assignments quarterly using Entra ID Access Reviews.
Request Microsoft Graph permissions at the narrowest scope; avoid .All permissions unless strictly necessary.
Avoid wildcard DataActions (*) in custom role definitions — enumerate permitted actions explicitly.
Terraform: Least-Privilege Role Assignments

Managing role assignments through Terraform makes least privilege enforceable via code review and prevents ad-hoc broad assignments from slipping through. The following module pattern creates a role assignment at the container scope with an optional ABAC condition.

# Usage: assign read access to a specific container with tag filter
module "app_storage_access" {
  source             = "./modules/storage-reader"
  principal_id       = azurerm_user_assigned_identity.app.principal_id
  storage_account_id = azurerm_storage_account.data.id
  container_name     = "app-uploads"
  abac_tag_key       = "environment"
  abac_tag_value     = "production"
}

Encoding role assignments in Terraform forces scope and role decisions to be reviewed in PRs, creates an audit trail in state history, and prevents manually granted broad assignments from persisting after infrastructure is rebuilt.
Entra ID Access Reviews

Role assignments accumulate over time as workloads change and are decommissioned. Entra ID Identity Governance provides access reviews that periodically challenge reviewers to confirm whether a principal still needs its assigned role. Automating this removes the manual burden of quarterly access audits.
Create an access review via the CLI

# Access Reviews are configured through the Entra portal or Graph API
# Below is a Graph API call using a service principal with AccessReview.ReadWrite.All

ACCESS_TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token \
  --resource https://graph.microsoft.com/ \
  --query accessToken -o tsv)

curl -s -X POST https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identityGovernance/accessReviews/definitions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $ACCESS_TOKEN" \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
    "displayName": "Quarterly SP Role Review",
    "scope": {
      "@odata.type": "#microsoft.graph.principalResourceMembershipsScope",
      "principalScopes": [{ "@odata.type": "#microsoft.graph.accessReviewQueryScope",
        "query": "/servicePrincipals", "queryType": "MicrosoftGraph" }],
      "resourceScopes": [{ "@odata.type": "#microsoft.graph.accessReviewQueryScope",
        "query": "/roleManagement/directory/roleAssignments", "queryType": "MicrosoftGraph" }]
    },
    "reviewers": [{ "query": "/users/admin@contoso.com", "queryType": "MicrosoftGraph" }],
    "settings": {
      "mailNotificationsEnabled": true,
      "reminderNotificationsEnabled": true,
      "justificationRequiredOnApproval": true,
      "defaultDecisionEnabled": true,
      "defaultDecision": "Deny",
      "instanceDurationInDays": 7,
      "recurrence": { "pattern": { "type": "absoluteMonthly", "interval": 3 },
        "range": { "type": "noEnd" } }
    }
  }'

The `defaultDecision: Deny` setting is critical: if a reviewer does not act within the review window, the role assignment is automatically revoked. This turns inaction into revocation rather than continuation — the safe default.
Detecting and Removing Excessive Permissions

Azure Advisor and Microsoft Defender for Cloud surface recommendations when a principal has a broad role assignment but has only used a narrow subset of its permissions. The Effective Permissions API and the Unused Access blade in Entra ID provide the same signal programmatically.
Find role assignments unused in the last 90 days
Azure Resource Graph: list all Contributor/Owner assignments

# Use Resource Graph to query role assignments at scale
az graph query -q "
  AuthorizationResources
  | where type == 'microsoft.authorization/roleassignments'
  | extend RoleDefId = tostring(properties.roleDefinitionId)
  | where RoleDefId endswith '8e3af657-a8ff-443c-a75c-2fe8c4bcb635'  // Owner
      or RoleDefId endswith 'b24988ac-6180-42a0-ab88-20f7382dd24c'  // Contributor
  | extend Scope = tostring(properties.scope)
  | extend Principal = tostring(properties.principalId)
  | project Principal, RoleDefId, Scope, id
  | order by Scope asc
" --query data -o table
Conditional Access Policies for Service Principals

Entra ID Conditional Access can apply to service principal sign-ins, not just user sign-ins. Workload Identities Conditional Access policies can require that an SP authenticates only from a specific IP range (e.g., your on-premises network or a known Azure region), blocking token acquisition from unexpected locations even if the credential is compromised.

Conditional Access for Workload Identities adds a network-level least-privilege control: even if a credential is stolen, the attacker cannot use it from an unrecognised IP or region. This is defence-in-depth on top of credential least privilege.
Least-Privilege Anti-Patterns to Avoid
Anti-pattern
Why it violates least privilege
Correct approach
Assigning Owner to a workload identity 'temporarily'
Owners can grant themselves further permissions; 'temporary' becomes permanent
Use Contributor or a specific data-plane role with a time-bound PIM assignment
Using a single SP for all CI/CD jobs
One compromised pipeline job has access to everything every other job can do
One SP (or WIF subject) per job/environment; separate prod and non-prod credentials
Granting Storage Blob Data Contributor at account scope because containers vary
Gives write access to every container including ones added in the future
Enumerate containers and assign at each container scope; use Terraform to manage
Requesting Directory.Read.All for a user lookup feature
Reads all users, groups, and directory objects in the tenant
Use User.Read.All scoped to specific users, or User.ReadBasic.All for name/email
Leaving unused role assignments in place after a project ends
Stale assignments accumulate and represent unmonitored access paths
Use access reviews with defaultDecision=Deny and clean up assignments on project close
Summary

Least privilege for Azure identities means assigning the narrowest role at the narrowest scope, using ABAC conditions to restrict within that scope, and auditing regularly for drift. Data-plane roles are almost always preferable to management-plane roles for workload identities. Custom roles fill gaps where no built-in role exactly fits. The goal is to ensure that a compromised identity — regardless of how it is compromised — can only affect the minimal set of resources it was intentionally granted access to.
